beats.text: TensorFlow launched in 2015 as Google's successor to DistBelief, and its founding bet was the computation graph.

visual.sub: Describe the computation, then run it

beats.text: You did not run arithmetic; you described it as a directed graph whose nodes are operations and whose edges are tensors.
A static graph lets the framework see the whole program first, so it can fuse ops, place devices, and serialize for deployment.
Each op records a forward output and a backward gradient, and the chain rule in reverse order is automatic differentiation.
TensorFlow 2.0 in 2019 flipped the default to eager execution while keeping graphs on demand through tf.function.
Graph mode pays a one-time tracing cost, then runs cheaply, so it wins once a step runs in a training loop.
Keras began in 2015 as a framework-agnostic frontend and became TensorFlow's official high-level API in version 2.0.
Its smallest unit is the layer; the workhorse Dense computes an affine transform followed by a nonlinearity.

visual.note: W is u by d_in, b has length u

beats.text: A Dense layer with u units on d_in features holds d_in times u weights plus u biases, totaling d_in plus one times u parameters.

visual.note: The +1 is the bias per unit

beats.text: Keras offers three ways to express a network: Sequential as a chain, Functional as a DAG, and Subclassing for dynamic control flow.
A Dense(64) layer reading 32 features holds 64 times 32 equals 2048 weights, before counting its 64 bias terms.
The classic MNIST classifier on 784 pixels carries about 118 thousand parameters, with the first layer dominating the count.
A model is only as fast as the data reaching it, and a starved accelerator sits idle while the CPU prepares batches.
If the GPU finishes in eight milliseconds but the CPU needs twenty, the accelerator idles roughly sixty percent of the time.
A pipeline chains transformations: map applies preprocessing, shuffle randomizes order, and batch groups examples together.
Prefetch overlaps preparing batch t plus one with training on batch t, turning a sum of times into a max.
With input and compute both equal to t, serial costs two t per step and overlapped costs t, a clean two times speedup.
With twelve milliseconds input and twenty compute, an overlapped step takes the max, twenty milliseconds, fully hiding input.
Once a model is built and data is ready, training is a loop, and Keras gives you that loop for free.

visual.sub: What model.fit does on your behalf

beats.text: After compile, one call to fit iterates batches, runs the forward pass, backpropagates loss, and applies the optimizer update.
What fit automates each batch is the gradient-descent step: average the loss, take the gradient, step downhill by eta.

visual.note: One fit step over minibatch B

beats.text: Callbacks observe and steer training without touching the loop: checkpoint best weights, stop early, reduce learning rate, log.
Overriding train_step keeps fit's outer machinery while replacing only the inner forward and backward logic.
A fully custom loop is the last resort, for multiple optimizers, gradient surgery, or research that inspects every step.

visual.label: fit lines you must write by hand

beats.text: Every team eventually asks which framework to use, and the honest answer starts with a fact about the field.
By conference citation share and new open-model releases, PyTorch has led research since roughly 2019 and 2020.
Both default to eager and compile to graphs, so torch.compile and tf.function now mirror each other closely.
TensorFlow still earns its place on three fronts: deployment breadth, native TPU support, and Keras 3 as a portability hedge.
Keras 3 runs on TensorFlow, JAX, or PyTorch behind one identical API, a genuinely new reason to consider it.
The frameworks have converged, so remaining differences are about ecosystem and deployment surface, not core capability.
